questions.question: Define proposition, atomic proposition, and compound proposition with examples.
keyPoints: Proposition: Declarative statement that is either true or false
Atomic proposition: Simple proposition without connectives (e.g., 'It is raining')
Compound proposition: Combination using connectives (e.g., 'It is raining AND it is cold')

questions.question: Construct truth tables for AND (∧), OR (∨), and IMPLICATION (→) connectives.
keyPoints: AND: T only when both T, F otherwise
OR: F only when both F, T otherwise
Implication: F only when T→F, T otherwise

questions.question: State and prove De Morgan's laws in propositional logic.
keyPoints: Proof via truth tables showing identical truth values

questions.question: Explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with examples.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: [(P → Q) ∧ P] ⇒ Q - If it rains, ground gets wet. It rained. Therefore ground is wet.
Modus Tollens: [(P → Q) ∧ ¬Q] ⇒ ¬P - If it rains, ground gets wet. Ground is not wet. Therefore it did not rain.

questions.question: Differentiate between tautology, contradiction, and contingency with examples.
keyPoints: Tautology: Always true (P ∨ ¬P)
Contradiction: Always false (P ∧ ¬P)
Contingency: Depends on truth values (P ∧ Q)

questions.question: Find the contrapositive, inverse, and converse of the proposition: 'If it is raining (P), then the ground is wet (Q).'
keyPoints: Contrapositive: If ground is not wet, then it is not raining (¬Q → ¬P)
Converse: If ground is wet, then it is raining (Q → P)
Inverse: If it is not raining, then ground is not wet (¬P → ¬Q)

questions.question: Using truth table method, prove that (P → Q) ≡ (¬P ∨ Q).
keyPoints: Construct truth table for both expressions
Show identical truth values for all combinations
Both are false only when P=T and Q=F
